episode_title: Fabian Heckenberg, Naja Nielsen & Gard Steiro: The Hard Truths About AI Every Newsroom Leader Can’t Ignore (Recorded Live at Nordic AI in Media Summit 2025)

theme_codings.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: So we need to find the journalism we do today that really can't be copied, that gives value to the readers, and we need to double down on that.
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: A central tension in the panel is identifying what AI will replace versus what remains uniquely human. Steiro argues newsrooms must identify irreplaceable journalism and shift resources toward it. Nielsen challenges the assumption that humans in the loop are always more trusted, citing surgery and autopilot examples, and argues machines may be both better and more trusted at certain tasks. Heckenberger insists on-the-ground reporters are still needed for major events. The panel converges on the idea that storytelling, human curiosity, and contextual reporting are irreducible, while routine content production and distribution will be increasingly automated.

theme_codings.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: I think honestly I think for most of us people are not in doubt that we are competent. They are in doubt about whether we want to do them well. Whether we really have their interests at our hearts
best_quote.speaker: Naja Nielsen

theme_codings.summary: Trust is discussed extensively as a design challenge for AI-powered news products. Steiro references an INLAB survey showing younger audiences value relevance as the primary driver of trust, not just brand reputation. Nielsen reframes the trust question: audiences doubt not competence but intent, and argues transparency and admitting errors are crucial. The panel discusses how personalization could actually increase trust by making content more relevant. They also address the question of whether AI outputs will eventually be more trusted than human outputs, drawing on analogies to medical robots and autopilots.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: It's very important to create kind of a transformative framework. To give the newsroom and the organization some guidelines. and to point out what is going on, what is happening, but also on the other hand what we don't know about
best_quote.speaker: Fabian Heckenberger

theme_codings.summary: Ethics infrastructure surfaces through the discussion of editorial guardrails and governance frameworks. Heckenberger advocates for a transformative framework that balances guidelines with spaces for experimentation. The human-in-the-loop debate touches editorial boundaries: Steiro argues you cannot maintain human oversight on everything if you want to personalize content, requiring new governance models. Nielsen notes that personalization must be aligned to values. The panel does not go deep into formal ethics policies but discusses the practical tension between editorial control and automation at scale.

theme_codings.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: You have one algorithm, you want but doing one task And then you set up another agent that do another task in newsroom to try to find the mistakes.
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: Technical architecture is only briefly touched upon. Steiro describes a multi-agent approach where one algorithm performs a task and another checks for errors, replacing the traditional human-in-the-loop model. The panel mentions VG's AI lab in Schibsted as infrastructure for running projects. However, the conversation stays at the strategic and organizational level rather than diving into technical implementation details like model selection, RAG, or build-versus-buy decisions.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: I think as top managers it's our responsibility as Eri from Helsingin Sanomat said yesterday, it's our responsibility to know what is going on, to be informed enough And to be brave enough to make the damned if you do, damned if you don't decisions.
best_quote.speaker: Naja Nielsen

theme_codings.summary: The panel emphasizes organizational learning and experimentation culture rather than formal training programs. Steiro describes VG's approach of including as many staff as possible in early AI experiments to build collective understanding. Nielsen stresses that top managers must be informed enough to make difficult decisions and should learn from Silicon Valley's working methods. Heckenberger discusses the need for testing spaces and freedom to experiment. The overall message is that literacy comes through hands-on participation rather than top-down instruction.

theme_codings.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: I think my most important task as a leader is not to point direction, but to foster a discussion within the organization.
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: Organizational transformation is arguably the dominant theme of the panel. Steiro describes leadership as fostering internal disagreement and scenario-based planning rather than dictating direction. Nielsen critiques the legacy assumption that the editor-in-chief must be the smartest person in the room, arguing strategic decisions get accidentally delegated while leaders focus on minutiae. Heckenberger describes SZ's interdisciplinary teams embedding journalists within product teams for homepage, apps, and audio. The panel discusses the prioritization battle between maintaining existing subscriber features and developing new AI capabilities, with Steiro describing VG's AI lab structure with clear one-year timelines and defined problem statements for projects.

theme_codings.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: I don't think that you can build a fence around Norway or around the EU or something like that and just protect you from what is happening now.
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: Platform power appears through the lens of competitive threat. Steiro warns that newsrooms testing products internally may develop something good while Silicon Valley built something better two years ago. He explicitly rejects protectionist strategies. Nielsen argues the industry needs to learn from Silicon Valley's ways of working rather than just viewing tech companies as adversaries. The closing remarks frame the discussion as an industry needing to collaborate against tech giants reshaping the information ecosystem. Content rights are touched on with the observation that facts are not copyrightable, threatening news business models.

theme_codings.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: I think we have to change the way we collect facts and gather it and structure it and then we can present articles for you if you want articles. But a lot of users would probably engage with that content in a different way.
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: Product reimagination is a major thread. The panel debates whether the article format should die, with Steiro arguing the article is already a legacy container from print that was merely ported to digital. Nielsen believes articles will persist for a small subset but the mothership of journalism must diversify into audio, conversation, and personalized formats. Heckenberger suggests thinking in phases about which article types die first, with news articles potentially already dead. Steiro describes the VGX project as a radical experiment in new formats for next-generation users. Personalization is framed as essential for relevance and trust, with Nielsen noting public service has debated it for a decade without acting while Finland's YLE is taking first steps.

theme_codings.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: I don't have the answers, and this is what keeps me awake every night. It's how should we find this journalism in the future?
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: Business model sustainability is a recurring anxiety throughout the panel. Steiro describes three ROI baskets: subscriber business measured by retention, efficiency gains measured in hours saved, and pure experimentation which he says should be phased out. He expresses deep concern about the ad market's viability for smaller players and notes most Nordic media companies are now foundation-owned, suggesting philanthropy may be part of the future. Heckenberger admits the subscriber model faces challenges but sees a short-term opportunity for quality journalism as content verification becomes harder. Nielsen raises the political precariousness of public service funding. An audience member poses the provocative question of what the penny press equivalent is for AI, which the panel cannot answer.

theme_codings.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Journalism as Accountability

best_quote.text: news as such, the article is copyrighted, but facts are not copyrighted, and that affects the business model of news.
best_quote.speaker: Nikita Roy

theme_codings.summary: Regulation receives minimal direct attention. Steiro explicitly says he tries not to focus on EU regulation because he cannot influence it, preferring to focus on what the newsroom can control. The copyright question surfaces briefly when Roy notes that facts are not copyrightable and mentions licensing deals and lawsuits. The panel does not engage deeply with the EU AI Act, platform regulation, or self-regulation frameworks, treating regulatory uncertainty as a background condition rather than a topic for strategic action.

theme_codings.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: I said this is the problem you're gonna solve, visa goals, and we give you one year. And if you don't have any results at one year, then we'll shut you down.
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: Steiro provides a concrete account of VG's experiment-to-product pipeline. The AI lab in Schibsted serves as an incubator with clear one-year timelines and defined problem statements. He describes the VGX project's user testing failure as a key lesson: testing new products on loyal super-users produces misleading feedback because they prefer existing formats. This reveals a failure pattern of selection bias in evaluation. Heckenberger raises the unresolved prioritization battle between building for current subscribers and testing new GenAI features. The panel discusses the challenge of knowing when experiments have succeeded, with Steiro emphasizing retention, loyalty, and appropriate KPIs over clicks.

theme_codings.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: how we structure our content, how we structure facts. And I think we'd have to think differently about that.
best_quote.speaker: Gard Steiro

theme_codings.summary: Data and privacy concerns are not a significant focus of this panel. The closest connection is Steiro's discussion of restructuring how newsrooms collect, gather, and structure facts and content to enable new presentation formats and personalization. This implies a data strategy shift but the panel does not address privacy regulations, source protection, content licensing arrangements, or synthetic data in any substantive way.
